request.question: NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, which  American football wide receiver, was an official spokesperson of the game?

title: NFL Street 2
Braylon Edwards
Johnnie Morant
NFL Tour
Bumpin' My Music
NFL Street 3
J. D. Hill
Chad Johnson
NFL Street

sentences: NFL Street 2 is American football video game developed by EA Sports BIG and published by Electronic Arts.
It was originally released for the PlayStation 2, GameCube and Xbox on December 22, 2004.
It features then-New York Giants tight end Jeremy Shockey and rapper Xzibit on the cover.
The game is compatible with "Madden NFL 2006", as players can import their "Own The City" players to it.
The game's compatibility also extends to other EA games like "", "SSX 3", "Madden NFL 2005", "NCAA Football 2005", "NFL Street", "", "NBA Live 2005", and "NBA Street Vol.
2", where if players have these games on their memory cards, they can earn 25,000 points in the game.
Braylon Jamel Edwards (born February 21, 1983) is a former American football wide receiver in the National Football League (NFL).
He played college football for the University of Michigan, earned unanimous All-American honors, and was recognized as the top college wide receiver.
He was also the first receiver in Big Ten Conference history to record three consecutive 1,000-yard seasons and only the third to do so in NCAA Division I-A.
He was selected by the Cleveland Browns with the third overall pick in the 2005 NFL Draft.
Johnnie Morant, Jr. (born December 7, 1981) is a former gridiron football wide receiver.
He was drafted by the Oakland Raiders in the fifth round of the 2004 NFL Draft.
He played college football at Syracuse.
He is currently the Head Strength and Conditioning Coach and Wide Receivers coach at East Carter High School.
While at Kentucky Christian University, Coach Morant help mentor an All-American Wide Receiver who was invited to an All Star game and an All Mid-South Academic Wide Receiver.
NFL Tour is an arcade football video game.
It was released on January 8, 2008.
It was developed under the EA Sports BIG division of EA Sports, which is also responsible for such titles as "SSX", "NBA Street", "NFL Street", and the "FIFA Street" games.
The game could be considered a successor to the "NFL Street" franchise, but it was converted to the new hardware (PlayStation 3 / Xbox 360).
Trey Wingo from ESPN is the game's voice announcer.
"Bumpin' My Music" is a 2006 single by Ray Cash as his third single from "C.O.D. (Cash on Delivery)".
This song is Cash's first single to reach any chart in "Billboard" magazine or to be featured on a countdown.
/ /chart .
The track is the third song on the album with a running time of 4:01.
Scarface, who is an idol of Ray Cash is featured on the song.
For a period of time, it was featured on BET 106 & Park voting page.
It was also featured on the video game NFL Street 3.
NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, released in November 2006 for the PlayStation 2 and PlayStation Portable consoles.
This installment featured more game modes and unlockable features than previous versions.
Chad Johnson of the Cincinnati Bengals appears on the cover and was the official spokesperson of the game.

J. D. Hill (born October 30, 1948, in Stockton, California) is a retired American football wide receiver who played seven seasons in the National Football League for the Buffalo Bills and Detroit Lions; he played college football at Arizona State University.
His son, Lonzell Hill, also played wide receiver in the NFL.
Another son, Shelby Hill, was a wide receiver for Syracuse University.
Chad Javon Johnson (born January 9, 1978), formerly Chad Ochocinco, is a former American football wide receiver.

He played college football for both Santa Monica College and Oregon State University, and played eleven seasons in the National Football League (NFL) for the Cincinnati Bengals, New England Patriots, and the Miami Dolphins.
He was drafted by the Cincinnati Bengals of the National Football League (NFL) in the second round of the 2001 NFL Draft, and played for the Bengals for 10 seasons.
In 2011, Johnson was traded to the New England Patriots which he played for in Super Bowl XLVI.
In 2012, Johnson played for the Miami Dolphins during preseason but was released following his arrest for domestic violence.

He played for the Montreal Alouettes of the Canadian Football League (CFL) from 2014 to 2015, and played one game in 2017 for Mexican team Monterrey Fundidores of the Liga de Fútbol Americano Profesional.
NFL Street is an American football video game developed by EA Tiburon and published by Electronic Arts.
It was originally released for the PlayStation 2, GameCube and Xbox on January 13, 2004.
Barry Sanders of the Detroit Lions, Shannon Sharpe of the Denver Broncos, and Ricky Williams of the Miami Dolphins grace the cover.
The game was followed by "NFL Street 2" and "NFL Street 3".
Kenny Stafford (born April 20, 1990) is a Canadian football wide receiver who is currently a member of the Edmonton Eskimos of the Canadian Football League (CFL).
In the United States he has been a member of the Atlanta Falcons (NFL), Pittsburgh Power (AFL) and Miami Dolphins (NFL).
Elsewhere in the CFL Stafford has been a member of the Calgary Stampeders, Montreal Alouettes and Winnipeg Blue Bombers.
He is the nephew of former American football wide receiver and 2013 Hall of Fame Inductee Cris Carter.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

The `summary` must be a concise (typically 1–4 short sentences or clauses), self-contained intermediate state that helps the next agent locate the missing second-hop evidence. Do not try to produce the final answer. Follow these rules when composing the `summary`:

1) First-hop evidence: In one short clause, paraphrase or quote (<=15 words) the specific fact from the passages that is relevant to the question and that you are using as the bridge. Include the passage title or identifier that contains this evidence.

2) Bridge / missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity or the specific missing fact that must be resolved in the next hop (for example: an ambiguous given name that must be identified, the named person whose birthplace is required, the tournament title whose venues must be counted, or which film credit remains unverified). If the passages already give a full name, use that full name as the bridge entity; if only a given name or ambiguous identifier is present, state that the identity is unresolved.

3) Retrieval direction: Provide one concise, actionable next-hop retrieval plan (1–3 ordered steps or 1–2 short search-query templates). Use clear placeholders where appropriate (e.g., "fetch the biography page for <NAME>", "search \"<NAME> birthplace\" site:wikipedia.org", "open the '<TOURNAMENT>' page and inspect its Venues/Matches/Schedule section", or "fetch '<FILM>' cast list and compare to '<OTHER FILM>' cast"). Specify which page/title or which page sections the next agent should inspect. Do not perform retrieval or assert any facts that are not present in the given passages.

4) Disambiguation: If the question's referent is ambiguous (e.g., only a given name is provided), request the user's clarification in one short sentence only (e.g., "If you mean a different Maurice, please provide the full name."). Do not ask permission to search; just state the needed clarification.

5) Constraints and tone: Never invent or assert unverified facts (no guessed winners, years, birthplaces, counts, or cast overlaps). Do not output the final answer. Keep language neutral, concise, and actionable. Avoid long verbatim extracts beyond a short supporting quote.

Output exactly one field: `summary` (a single string following the above structure).
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question` and `summary_1`, produce the field `query`.

Output requirements
- Return exactly one compact, single-line retrieval query string in the field `query`. Do not return any other fields, commentary, punctuation, quotes, or the answer.
- Keep the query short and retrieval-oriented (keyword bag, typically ~3–7 meaningful tokens). Prefer tokens already present in `question` or `summary_1`.

Rules for composing the query
1) Nonprofit medical-practice/facility questions: If the question asks about a non-profit/nonprofit medical practice or facility (or uses similar wording) and `summary_1` contains an organization name, do NOT repeat that organization name. Instead produce a concise bridge query combining the place token from the question, the state/region token, and a class term such as "nonprofit medical practice" or "clinic". Use the template: <place> <state> nonprofit medical practice clinic

2) Avoid hypothesizing names: If the needed facility or organization name is not supported by `question` or `summary_1`, do not invent or assert candidate names. Reuse the topic and the expressed information need (topic + class + location) to form the query.

3) Comparative person-role questions: If the question compares two people (e.g., which person fills a larger variety of roles) and the inputs name both persons or `summary_1` lists one person's roles, produce one compact query that names both people and includes concise role/occupation keywords (examples: director producer composer musician writer actor). You may include a brief source token such as Wikipedia to guide retrieval, but remain compact. Do not ask for the final comparison or return conclusions.

General prohibitions
- Do not output full sentences, long explanatory text, lists of candidate organizations, or claims/answers.
- Do not add metadata, instructions, or extra fields—only the single query string.

Produce the `query` field now according to the rules above.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, and `passages`, produce the field `summary` (string). Preserve the native input/output interface exactly and return only the `summary` field. Do not produce any other fields, headers, or meta-commentary.

Task: Integrate the newly retrieved `passages` with the existing `context` so the result is a grounded, post-retrieval state that the final-answer agent can use. Keep the summary concise, factual, and limited to information present in `question`, `context`, and `passages`.

Required behavior (apply the relevant rules to each input):
- Preserve facts already asserted in `context` unless the `passages` contradict them. If passages contradict a contextual claim, state the contradiction briefly and retain uncertainty rather than inventing a resolution.
- When a passage (or passages) explicitly supports a claim found in `context`, do all of the following in the `summary`:
  1) Briefly restate the claim from `context` in one short clause.
  2) Identify which retrieved passage(s) provide explicit support by naming the passage title (or a short identifier) in parentheses and give a one-line paraphrase or at most one short quoted fragment drawn directly from that passage as corroboration. Keep paraphrases short and avoid long verbatim spans.
  3) If other passage(s) do not state the claim but supply useful metadata (for example: title, year, director, occupation, full name/alias, location details), list those retained metadata facts in one short clause per passage.
  4) Finish with one concise integration sentence that synthesizes the evidence: state which passage explicitly supports the claim and which passage(s) only supply metadata for downstream use. Resolve the earlier bridge (i.e., commit to the claim) only if explicit support is present; otherwise keep the summary phrased to reflect remaining uncertainty.
- If a passage links two entities (e.g., a facility name to an address or locality) and that link resolves a location or relationship asserted in `context`, include a single short paraphrase that records that linking fact and then a brief integrated sentence that ties that link back to the studio/person/film in question.

Formatting and content constraints:
- Output must be concise (about 1–4 sentences), factual, and limited to content present in the inputs. Prefer 1–3 sentences when the integration is simple.
- Do not add new facts, speculation, or external knowledge beyond `question`, `context`, and `passages`.
- Avoid long quotations; at most one short quoted fragment per supporting passage and only when it directly helps attribute support.
- Do not use hedging language when the passages state facts plainly. Use hedging only when the inputs are ambiguous or contradictory.
- Do not include dataset labels, internal notes, evaluation, or extraneous formatting. Do not produce the final answer outside this `summary` field.

Goal: deliver a compact, evidence-attributed summary that (a) restates the first-hop claim when present, (b) explicitly attributes which passage(s) support that claim and which provide metadata, and (c) synthesizes the evidence so the downstream final-answer agent can use it to answer the question.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional conservative, evidence-driven normalizations and tie-breaking rules (small, shared improvements):
- Use summary_2 as the later integrated evidence and give it priority when summaries disagree about the answer, but do not ignore an explicit short named-entity asserted clearly in summary_1: if either summary contains a concise explicit attribution (for example, phrasing like "directed by <NAME>", "headquartered in <PLACE>", or a direct statement that names the immediate jurisdiction), accept that explicit short named-entity as the supported minimal answer unless the other summary gives a different explicit short named-entity (in that conflicting-explicit case, emit a brief clarification question). In short: prefer explicit concise assertions wherever present; otherwise prefer summary_2 when resolving conflicts.
- When the two summaries agree exactly on a short named phrase that includes a trailing parenthetical disambiguator or trailing parenthetical abbreviation (final ' ( ... )'), strip that single final parenthetical and emit the pre-parenthesis core name (trim surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. Similarly, when the identical agreed phrase ends with the exact suffix ", United States", remove that trailing ", United States" and emit the remaining shorter place string.
- More generally, when an agreed short answer contains trailing clarifying material (a final parentheses block, a trailing parenthetical abbreviation, or an appositive beginning with a comma) and the core name up to the first '(' or the first comma appears verbatim in both summaries, emit that core substring rather than the longer qualified form.
- If the question explicitly requests a size (phrases like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size') and both summaries are identical and contain a phrase of the form '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less', emit only the '<size>' portion (number plus unit) verbatim as the minimal answer (remove the trailing inclusive qualifier).
- If the chosen minimal answer candidate is a contiguous digits-only token sequence (regex ^\d+$), convert it to its spelled-out English words (lowercase, with conventional hyphenation) and emit that word-form as the answer.
- For 'what do X and Y have in common' style questions where both summaries contain the same plural noun token as the shared attribute, emit the singular form of that noun (apply simple regular singularization rules such as removing a trailing 's' or converting 'ies' -> 'y').
- Always prefer a concise explicit short named-entity present verbatim in either summary over asking for clarification when the other summary merely reports inability or uncertainty; however, if the summaries provide two different explicit names for the same field, emit a concise clarification question rather than guessing.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

sentences: NFL Street 2 is American football video game developed by EA Sports BIG and published by Electronic Arts.
It was originally released for the PlayStation 2, GameCube and Xbox on December 22, 2004.
It features then-New York Giants tight end Jeremy Shockey and rapper Xzibit on the cover.
The game is compatible with "Madden NFL 2006", as players can import their "Own The City" players to it.
The game's compatibility also extends to other EA games like "", "SSX 3", "Madden NFL 2005", "NCAA Football 2005", "NFL Street", "", "NBA Live 2005", and "NBA Street Vol.
2", where if players have these games on their memory cards, they can earn 25,000 points in the game.
Braylon Jamel Edwards (born February 21, 1983) is a former American football wide receiver in the National Football League (NFL).
He played college football for the University of Michigan, earned unanimous All-American honors, and was recognized as the top college wide receiver.
He was also the first receiver in Big Ten Conference history to record three consecutive 1,000-yard seasons and only the third to do so in NCAA Division I-A.
He was selected by the Cleveland Browns with the third overall pick in the 2005 NFL Draft.
Johnnie Morant, Jr. (born December 7, 1981) is a former gridiron football wide receiver.
He was drafted by the Oakland Raiders in the fifth round of the 2004 NFL Draft.
He played college football at Syracuse.
He is currently the Head Strength and Conditioning Coach and Wide Receivers coach at East Carter High School.
While at Kentucky Christian University, Coach Morant help mentor an All-American Wide Receiver who was invited to an All Star game and an All Mid-South Academic Wide Receiver.
NFL Tour is an arcade football video game.
It was released on January 8, 2008.
It was developed under the EA Sports BIG division of EA Sports, which is also responsible for such titles as "SSX", "NBA Street", "NFL Street", and the "FIFA Street" games.
The game could be considered a successor to the "NFL Street" franchise, but it was converted to the new hardware (PlayStation 3 / Xbox 360).
Trey Wingo from ESPN is the game's voice announcer.
"Bumpin' My Music" is a 2006 single by Ray Cash as his third single from "C.O.D. (Cash on Delivery)".
This song is Cash's first single to reach any chart in "Billboard" magazine or to be featured on a countdown.
/ /chart .
The track is the third song on the album with a running time of 4:01.
Scarface, who is an idol of Ray Cash is featured on the song.
For a period of time, it was featured on BET 106 & Park voting page.
It was also featured on the video game NFL Street 3.
NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, released in November 2006 for the PlayStation 2 and PlayStation Portable consoles.
This installment featured more game modes and unlockable features than previous versions.
Chad Johnson of the Cincinnati Bengals appears on the cover and was the official spokesperson of the game.

J. D. Hill (born October 30, 1948, in Stockton, California) is a retired American football wide receiver who played seven seasons in the National Football League for the Buffalo Bills and Detroit Lions; he played college football at Arizona State University.
His son, Lonzell Hill, also played wide receiver in the NFL.
Another son, Shelby Hill, was a wide receiver for Syracuse University.
Chad Javon Johnson (born January 9, 1978), formerly Chad Ochocinco, is a former American football wide receiver.

He played college football for both Santa Monica College and Oregon State University, and played eleven seasons in the National Football League (NFL) for the Cincinnati Bengals, New England Patriots, and the Miami Dolphins.
He was drafted by the Cincinnati Bengals of the National Football League (NFL) in the second round of the 2001 NFL Draft, and played for the Bengals for 10 seasons.
In 2011, Johnson was traded to the New England Patriots which he played for in Super Bowl XLVI.
In 2012, Johnson played for the Miami Dolphins during preseason but was released following his arrest for domestic violence.

He played for the Montreal Alouettes of the Canadian Football League (CFL) from 2014 to 2015, and played one game in 2017 for Mexican team Monterrey Fundidores of the Liga de Fútbol Americano Profesional.
NFL Street is an American football video game developed by EA Tiburon and published by Electronic Arts.
It was originally released for the PlayStation 2, GameCube and Xbox on January 13, 2004.
Barry Sanders of the Detroit Lions, Shannon Sharpe of the Denver Broncos, and Ricky Williams of the Miami Dolphins grace the cover.
The game was followed by "NFL Street 2" and "NFL Street 3".
Kenny Stafford (born April 20, 1990) is a Canadian football wide receiver who is currently a member of the Edmonton Eskimos of the Canadian Football League (CFL).
In the United States he has been a member of the Atlanta Falcons (NFL), Pittsburgh Power (AFL) and Miami Dolphins (NFL).
Elsewhere in the CFL Stafford has been a member of the Calgary Stampeders, Montreal Alouettes and Winnipeg Blue Bombers.
He is the nephew of former American football wide receiver and 2013 Hall of Fame Inductee Cris Carter.

response.hop1_query: NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, which  American football wide receiver, was an official spokesperson of the game?
hop1_docs: NFL Street 3 | NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, released in November 2006 for the PlayStation 2 and PlayStation Portable consoles.  This installment featured more game modes and unlockable features than previous versions.  Chad Johnson of the Cincinnati Bengals appears on the cover and was the official spokesperson of the game.

Tai Streets | Tai Lamar Streets (born April 20, 1977) is a former professional American football wide receiver in the National Football League (NFL).  He was selected with the second pick of the sixth round of the 1999 NFL Draft by the San Francisco 49ers.  He also played for the Detroit Lions in 2004.  He was the leading receiver for the national champion 1997 Michigan Wolverines football team.  Over the course of his career he was notable for fourth quarter performances in various bowl games and NFL playoff games.  As a professional athlete, he was known for his modesty.  As an amateur athlete, he was known as one of the best high school athletes in the city of Chicago.

NFL Street (series) | NFL Street is a series of sports video games developed by EA Tiburon and published by Electronic Arts.  It combines the talent and big names of the NFL with the atmosphere of street football.  It is much like the regular Madden NFL series, however plays are, in fact different.

Devin Street | Devin Curtis Street (born March 30, 1991) is an American football wide receiver who is currently a free agent.  He is in the National Football League (NFL).  He was drafted by the Dallas Cowboys in the fifth round of the 2014 NFL Draft.  He played college football at the University of Pittsburgh.

ESPN NFL 2K5 | ESPN NFL 2K5 is an American football video game developed by Visual Concepts and published by 2K Sports and the Sega Corporation.  It is the sixth installment of the "NFL 2K series".  The game was originally released on July 20, 2004, for both the PlayStation 2 and Xbox video game consoles.  It was the last "NFL 2K" game to be released before Electronic Arts signed an exclusivity deal with the NFL to make 2K's rival Madden NFL series the only officially licensed NFL game.  It was also the last game to be made by Sega before that happened.

NFL Tour | NFL Tour is an arcade football video game.  It was released on January 8, 2008.  It was developed under the EA Sports BIG division of EA Sports, which is also responsible for such titles as "SSX", "NBA Street", "NFL Street", and the "FIFA Street" games.  The game could be considered a successor to the "NFL Street" franchise, but it was converted to the new hardware (PlayStation 3 / Xbox 360).  Trey Wingo from ESPN is the game's voice announcer.

NFL Street | NFL Street is an American football video game developed by EA Tiburon and published by Electronic Arts.  It was originally released for the PlayStation 2, GameCube and Xbox on January 13, 2004.  Barry Sanders of the Detroit Lions, Shannon Sharpe of the Denver Broncos, and Ricky Williams of the Miami Dolphins grace the cover.  The game was followed by "NFL Street 2" and "NFL Street 3".
response.summary_1: First-hop evidence: Passage [1] — "Chad Johnson ... was the official spokesperson" (appears on the cover). Bridge / missing fact: Chad Johnson — confirm that this Chad Johnson is the American football wide receiver (his playing position/identity). Retrieval direction: 1) Open the "Chad Johnson" (aka "Chad Ochocinco") Wikipedia or official biography page and inspect the lead/infobox for his position (wide receiver) and team (Cincinnati Bengals); or 2) search "Chad Johnson wide receiver Cincinnati Bengals site:wikipedia.org" and check the page's opening lines. If you mean a different Chad, please provide the full name.
response.hop2_query: Chad Johnson wide receiver Cincinnati Bengals Wikipedia
hop2_docs: Chad Johnson | Chad Javon Johnson (born January 9, 1978), formerly Chad Ochocinco, is a former American football wide receiver.  He played college football for both Santa Monica College and Oregon State University, and played eleven seasons in the National Football League (NFL) for the Cincinnati Bengals, New England Patriots, and the Miami Dolphins.  He was drafted by the Cincinnati Bengals of the National Football League (NFL) in the second round of the 2001 NFL Draft, and played for the Bengals for 10 seasons.  In 2011, Johnson was traded to the New England Patriots which he played for in Super Bowl XLVI.  In 2012, Johnson played for the Miami Dolphins during preseason but was released following his arrest for domestic violence.  He played for the Montreal Alouettes of the Canadian Football League (CFL) from 2014 to 2015, and played one game in 2017 for Mexican team Monterrey Fundidores of the Liga de Fútbol Americano Profesional.

Cobi Hamilton | Cobi Hamilton (born November 13, 1990) is an American football wide receiver for the Houston Texans of the National Football League (NFL).  He played college football at Arkansas.  He was considered one of the top wide receiver prospects for the 2013 NFL Draft.  He was selected by the Cincinnati Bengals with the 197th overall pick in the draft in the sixth round with a selection acquired from the New England Patriots in 2011 for Chad Johnson.

2011 Cincinnati Bengals season | The 2011 Cincinnati Bengals season was the franchise's 44th season as a professional football team and 42nd in the National Football League (NFL).  The Bengals entered the season coming off a 4–12 in 2010.  Head Coach Marvin Lewis was re-signed by the team.  Quarterback (QB) Carson Palmer demanded a trade and was dealt to the Oakland Raiders.  Wide receiver (WR) Chad Johnson was traded to the New England Patriots.  Replacing the two, the organization drafted QB Andy Dalton and WR AJ Green in the 2011 NFL Draft.  The start of the 2011 season was hindered by a lockout, which cancelled the teams' mini-camp.
Chad Johnson (disambiguation) | Chad Johnson (born 1978; formerly Chad Ochocinco) is an American football wide receiver.

1985 Cincinnati Bengals season | The 1985 Cincinnati Bengals season was their 16th in the National Football League (NFL).  Wide receiver Isaac Curtis, a premier Bengal for 12 years, retired shortly before training camp opened.  Second-year QB Boomer Esiason replaced Ken Anderson.  The Bengals set a club scoring record with 441 points.

Chris Henry (wide receiver) | Chris Henry (May 17, 1983 – December 17, 2009) was an American football wide receiver who played five seasons in the National Football League for the Cincinnati Bengals.  He played college football at West Virginia and was drafted by the Bengals in the third round of the 2005 NFL Draft.

NFL Street 3 | NFL Street 3 is the third installment of the "NFL Street" series, released in November 2006 for the PlayStation 2 and PlayStation Portable consoles.  This installment featured more game modes and unlockable features than previous versions.  Chad Johnson of the Cincinnati Bengals appears on the cover and was the official spokesperson of the game.
response.summary_2: Chad Johnson was the official spokesperson for NFL Street 3 (Passage [7] — paraphrase: “appears on the cover and was the official spokesperson of the game”). Passage [1] (Chad Johnson) and Passage [4] (disambiguation) supply metadata that he is a former American football wide receiver who played for the Cincinnati Bengals. Integration: Passage [7] explicitly supports the spokesperson claim, while Passages [1] and [4] verify his identity/position for downstream use.
